test_rationale: This test isolates the underspecification by using one UPDATE that first deletes the only A RR at w.campus.edu. and then adds a CNAME at the same owner name. If the server executes the Update Section as a program, the final state is a valid CNAME-only node. If it instead normalizes or validates the packet as a whole, it may refuse the request or preserve the original A state. The follow-up CNAME and A queries make the difference observable as both reply-code and post-state divergence without requiring concurrency or multiple UPDATEs.

group_1.behavior: Processes the UPDATE sequentially. The delete_rr removes the A RR, then add_rr creates the CNAME, yielding a CNAME-only state.
group_1.post_state: w.campus.edu. has CNAME target.campus.edu. and no A RR.

group_2.behavior: Treats the UPDATE Section as a normalized set of actions or rejects the conflicting same-name/type transition within one packet.
group_2.post_state: w.campus.edu. remains A-only, or no CNAME is created.

expected_divergence.why_divergence: RFC 2136 §2 and §3.4.2 define the Update Section as a list of RRs and specify the kinds of updates, but they do not normatively state whether a server must apply those RRs strictly in wire order or may first normalize the packet into a set of actions. That leaves the outcome of a same-packet delete-then-add transition at one owner name underspecified. RFC 2181 §5.5 addresses RR and RRset ordering in other contexts, not UPDATE semantics.
